terms.definition: Seen from above, as if looking down from the sky; used to describe a type of photograph or view.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin aerius, of the air
terms.example_usage: The aerial photograph showed our school and the fields around it.

terms.definition: The second-largest continent, located south of Europe and surrounded by the Atlantic and Indian Oceans.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: Possibly from Latin Afer, referring to North African peoples
terms.example_usage: Africa is one of the seven continents and includes many different countries.
terms.common_errors: Thinking Africa is a country rather than a continent

terms.definition: The southernmost continent, covered in ice and surrounding the South Pole.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Greek antarktikos, opposite the Arctic
terms.example_usage: Antarctica is the coldest continent on Earth and has no permanent human population.

terms.definition: The region around the North Pole, including the Arctic Ocean and surrounding lands.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Greek arktikos, near the Bear (constellation)
terms.example_usage: The Arctic is very cold and is home to animals such as polar bears.

terms.definition: The largest continent, stretching from the Middle East to the Pacific Ocean.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Greek Asia, possibly from Assyrian asu, to go out (sunrise)
terms.example_usage: Asia is the biggest continent and includes countries like China, India, and Japan.

terms.definition: The ocean that lies between the Americas to the west and Europe and Africa to the east.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Greek Atlantikos, named after the mythological Atlas
terms.example_usage: Ships cross the Atlantic Ocean to travel between Europe and America.

terms.definition: A book of maps showing different countries, regions, and features of the world.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: Named after Atlas, the Greek Titan who held up the sky
terms.example_usage: We used an atlas to find where France is in relation to the United Kingdom.

terms.definition: A continent and country in the Southern Hemisphere, surrounded by the Indian and Pacific Oceans.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Australia is both a continent and a country in the Southern Hemisphere.

terms.definition: The season between summer and winter when temperatures cool and leaves change colour and fall.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: In autumn, the days get shorter and the weather becomes cooler.

terms.definition: An area of sand or pebbles along the edge of the sea or a lake.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The beach is a physical feature found along the coast.

terms.definition: The capital city of Northern Ireland, part of the United Kingdom.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Irish Beal Feirste, mouth of the sandbar
terms.example_usage: Belfast is the capital city of Northern Ireland.

terms.definition: The line or boundary that separates one country, region, or area from another.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The border between England and Scotland runs from coast to coast.

terms.definition: The main city of a country or region where the government is based.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin capitalis, of the head
terms.example_usage: London is the capital city of England and the United Kingdom.
terms.common_errors: Confused with capital letter or money

terms.definition: The capital city of Wales, part of the United Kingdom.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Cardiff is the capital city of Wales.

terms.definition: The qualities or features that describe what a place is like.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The characteristics of a seaside town include a harbour and beaches.

terms.definition: A large and important town, often with a cathedral and many different services.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: A city is bigger than a town and usually has more shops and buildings.

terms.definition: A steep rock face, especially at the edge of the sea or along a river valley.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The white cliffs along the coast are made of chalk.

terms.definition: The usual pattern of weather conditions in an area over a long period of time.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The climate in the UK is temperate, with mild winters and cool summers.
terms.common_errors: Confusing climate (long-term patterns) with weather (day-to-day conditions)

terms.definition: A visible mass of water droplets or ice crystals suspended in the atmosphere.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Old English clud, rock or hill
terms.example_usage: Dark clouds often mean that rain is on the way.

terms.definition: The area where the land meets the sea.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin costa, rib or side
terms.example_usage: The coast of Britain has cliffs, beaches, and harbours.

terms.definition: Having a low temperature; used in geography to describe weather conditions or climate.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The weather is colder in winter than in summer.

terms.definition: A group of people living in the same area who share spaces, services, and often a sense of belonging.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Our community uses the local park, shops, and school.

terms.definition: To look at two or more things to find similarities and differences between them.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin comparare, to pair together
terms.example_usage: We compared our village with a seaside town to see how they are different.

terms.definition: A tool or diagram showing the four main directions: north, south, east, and west.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: We used a compass to find which direction north was.
terms.common_errors: Confusing compass directions with a drawing compass

terms.definition: One of the seven large continuous areas of land on Earth: Africa, Antarctica, Asia, Australasia, Europe, North America, South America.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: There are seven continents in the world, and we live on the continent of Europe.

terms.definition: To show how two or more places, features, or conditions are different from each other.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: We contrasted life in a city with life in a village.

terms.definition: Moderately cold; used to describe weather that is not warm but not very cold.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The weather in spring is often cool rather than warm.

terms.definition: A nation with its own government, borders, and identity.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: France is a country in Europe, next to the United Kingdom.

terms.definition: The ways in which two or more things are not alike.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: We found differences between the weather in London and the weather in Cairo.

terms.definition: The way something is pointing or the path someone is travelling, often described using compass points.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The school is in the direction of north from our house.

terms.definition: The direction where the sun rises; one of the four main compass points.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The sun rises in the east every morning.

terms.definition: The capital city of Scotland, part of the United Kingdom.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Old English Edin, possibly meaning fort on a slope
terms.example_usage: Edinburgh is the capital city of Scotland.

terms.definition: The largest country in the United Kingdom, located in the southern part of the island of Great Britain.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Old English Englaland, land of the Angles
terms.example_usage: England is one of the four countries that make up the United Kingdom.
terms.common_errors: Confusing England with the United Kingdom or Great Britain

terms.definition: The narrow body of water between southern England and northern France.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The English Channel separates England from France.

terms.definition: The natural and human-made surroundings in which people, animals, and plants live.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Old French environner, to surround
terms.example_usage: We should look after our environment by not dropping litter.

terms.definition: An imaginary line around the middle of the Earth, equally distant from the North and South Poles.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin aequare, to make equal
terms.example_usage: Countries near the equator are usually hot all year round.

terms.definition: A continent in the Northern Hemisphere that includes countries such as France, Germany, and the United Kingdom.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The United Kingdom is part of the continent of Europe.

terms.definition: A building where goods are made or assembled, usually using machines.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: A factory is a human feature because it was built by people.

terms.definition: An area of land used for growing crops or raising animals for food.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The farm is in the countryside and has fields of wheat.

terms.definition: A noticeable part or characteristic of a place, which can be physical (natural) or human (built).
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Rivers and mountains are physical features; roads and buildings are human features.

terms.definition: A prediction about what the weather will be like in the near future, based on data and observations.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The weather forecast said it would rain tomorrow.

terms.definition: A large area of land covered mainly by trees and undergrowth.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: A forest is a physical feature with many trees growing close together.

terms.definition: A spherical model of the Earth showing the continents, oceans, and other features.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: We looked at the globe to find where Australia is.

terms.definition: A pattern of horizontal and vertical lines on a map, used to locate places accurately.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The grid on the map helps us find exact locations.

terms.definition: A sheltered area of water where ships can dock safely, often part of a coastal settlement.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The harbour is full of fishing boats that go out to sea each morning.

terms.definition: An area of land that is higher than the land around it, but not as tall as a mountain.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: We climbed the hill to get a better view of the valley below.

terms.definition: Having a high temperature; used in geography to describe warm weather or tropical climates.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Countries near the equator have hot weather all year round.

terms.definition: A building where people live, which is a human feature of any settlement.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Houses are human features that we find in towns and villages.

terms.definition: Made or caused by people, as opposed to occurring naturally; used to describe features of a place.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Roads, bridges, and shops are all human features of a town.

terms.definition: The ocean between Africa, Asia, and Australia.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The Indian Ocean lies between Africa and Australia.

terms.definition: The body of water between Ireland and Great Britain.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The Irish Sea separates Ireland from England and Wales.

terms.definition: A list of symbols used on a map with explanations of what each one represents.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The key on the map shows that a blue line means a river.

terms.definition: The visible features of an area of land, including hills, rivers, fields, and buildings.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The landscape around our school includes fields, a river, and a church.

terms.definition: Imaginary horizontal lines on a map or globe measuring distance north or south of the equator.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Lines of latitude run around the Earth from east to west.

terms.definition: The part of a map that explains what the symbols and colours mean.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin legenda, things to be read
terms.example_usage: The legend tells us that green areas on the map are parks.

terms.definition: The neighbourhood or community immediately surrounding where you live or go to school.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: We drew a map of our local area showing the school, shops, and park.

terms.definition: The particular place or position where something is found.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The location of our school is next to the park.

terms.definition: The capital city of England and the United Kingdom, situated on the River Thames.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: London is the capital city of the United Kingdom.

terms.definition: A drawing or diagram that shows what a place looks like from above, using symbols for different features.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin mappa, napkin or cloth
terms.example_usage: We used a map to find the way from school to the library.

terms.definition: A very high area of land with steep sides, much taller than a hill.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Ben Nevis is the tallest mountain in the United Kingdom.

terms.definition: Relating to countries, continents, or cultures outside Europe.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: We studied a non-European country to compare it with the United Kingdom.

terms.definition: The direction towards the North Pole; one of the four main compass points.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Scotland is to the north of England.

terms.definition: The continent that includes Canada, the United States, Mexico, and Central America.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: Named after Italian explorer Amerigo Vespucci
terms.example_usage: North America is the continent where the United States is located.

terms.definition: The body of water between Great Britain, Scandinavia, and mainland Europe.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The North Sea lies to the east of England.

terms.definition: One of the four countries of the United Kingdom, located in the northeast of the island of Ireland.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Northern Ireland is part of the United Kingdom but is on a different island from England.
terms.common_errors: Confusing Northern Ireland with the Republic of Ireland

terms.definition: A very large body of salt water; there are five oceans covering most of the Earths surface.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The Pacific Ocean is the largest ocean in the world.
terms.common_errors: Confusing ocean with sea; oceans are larger

terms.definition: A building or room where people work, typically at desks with computers.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: An office is a human feature found in towns and cities.

terms.definition: The largest and deepest ocean, lying between Asia and the Americas.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The Pacific Ocean is the biggest ocean on Earth.

terms.definition: Occurring naturally in the environment, not made by people; used to describe natural features of a place.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Rivers, mountains, and forests are physical features.

terms.definition: A specific area or location, which can be described by its physical and human features.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Every place has features that make it different from other places.

terms.definition: A view of something from directly above, looking straight down, as shown on most maps.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: A plan view of the classroom shows where the desks and chairs are arranged.

terms.definition: The two points at the very top and bottom of the Earth: the North Pole and the South Pole.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin polus, end of an axis
terms.example_usage: The North Pole and the South Pole are the coldest places on Earth.

terms.definition: A town or city with a harbour where ships load and unload goods or passengers.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Liverpool is a port city where ships have traded goods for centuries.

terms.definition: The amount of rain that falls in a particular area over a period of time.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The rainfall in the west of Britain is higher than in the east.

terms.definition: To show or stand for something using symbols, colours, or other methods on a map or diagram.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin repraesentare, to present again
terms.example_usage: The blue colour on the map represents water.

terms.definition: A large natural flow of water that travels through the land towards the sea or a lake.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin riparius, of a riverbank
terms.example_usage: The River Thames flows through London to the sea.

terms.definition: The relationship between the size of something on a map and its actual size in real life.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The scale on the map shows that one centimetre equals one kilometre.

terms.definition: One of the four countries of the United Kingdom, located in the northern part of Great Britain.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Latin Scotia, land of the Scots
terms.example_usage: Scotland is to the north of England.

terms.definition: A large body of salt water, smaller than an ocean, often partly enclosed by land.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The North Sea is to the east of England.

terms.definition: One of the four periods of the year (spring, summer, autumn, winter) each with typical weather patterns.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Each season brings different weather to the United Kingdom.

terms.definition: A place where people live, from a single farmstead to a large city.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Our village is a small settlement with about 200 houses.

terms.definition: A building where goods are sold; a human feature found in most settlements.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Shops are human features that we find on the high street.

terms.definition: The ways in which two or more things are alike.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: We found similarities between our town and a town in France.

terms.definition: The top layer of earth in which plants grow, made of minerals, organic matter, and water.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Different areas have different types of soil.

terms.definition: The direction towards the South Pole; one of the four main compass points.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: London is to the south of Birmingham.

terms.definition: The continent located mainly in the Southern and Western Hemispheres, including Brazil and Argentina.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: South America is the continent where the Amazon rainforest is found.

terms.definition: Situated in or towards the south part of a region, country, or area.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Southern England is generally warmer than northern England.

terms.definition: The season between winter and summer when temperatures rise and plants begin to grow.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.etymology: From Old English springan, to leap
terms.example_usage: In spring, flowers start to bloom and the days get longer.

terms.definition: The warmest season of the year, between spring and autumn.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Summer is the warmest season and when the days are longest.

terms.definition: The light and warmth from the sun reaching the Earths surface.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: We measured how many hours of sunshine we had each day this week.

terms.definition: A small picture or shape used on a map to represent a real feature, explained in the key.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The map uses a blue symbol to show where the river is.

terms.definition: A measure of how hot or cold something is, often recorded in degrees Celsius.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The temperature today is 15 degrees Celsius, which is quite cool.

terms.definition: A settlement larger than a village but smaller than a city, with shops and services.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Our town has a high street with shops, a library, and a post office.

terms.definition: A country made up of four nations: England, Scotland, Wales, and Northern Ireland.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The United Kingdom is made up of four countries.
terms.common_errors: Confusing UK with England or Great Britain

terms.definition: A low area of land between hills or mountains, often with a river flowing through it.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The river flows through the valley between the two mountains.

terms.definition: The plant life growing in a particular area, including trees, grass, and flowers.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The vegetation in a rainforest is very different from the vegetation in a desert.

terms.definition: A small settlement in the countryside, smaller than a town.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: A village usually has fewer shops and services than a town.

terms.definition: One of the four countries of the United Kingdom, located to the west of England.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Wales is to the west of England and has its own language, Welsh.

terms.definition: Having a comfortable level of heat; not too hot and not cold.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The weather is warm in summer in the United Kingdom.

terms.definition: The conditions in the atmosphere at a particular time and place, including temperature, rain, and wind.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The weather today is sunny with a few clouds.

terms.definition: The direction where the sun sets; one of the four main compass points.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The sun sets in the west every evening.

terms.definition: The movement of air from areas of high pressure to areas of low pressure.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: The wind was blowing strongly from the west today.

terms.definition: The coldest season of the year, between autumn and spring.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: Winter is the coldest season and when the days are shortest.

terms.definition: A flat representation of the entire Earths surface, showing all continents and oceans.
terms.subject: Geography
terms.example_usage: We looked at the world map to find where the seven continents are.
